acceptance_targets: [CH18_OVERSTATEMENT_BAO_NOTES] Pass if snapshots/cycle_02/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3595-3601 moves from the specimen notes to the confession attempt without a narratorial passage that restates the significance of Bao's writing practice in thematic language. Physical observation of the notes (weight, handwriting, stack height) is fine; abstract restatement of what the notes represent ('a man who had seen more than the system wanted') is not. Fail if the revised passage still contains a multi-clause interpretive summary of what Bao's notes mean after the notes themselves have already shown it.

[EM_DASH_CH18] Pass if revised chapter contains no more than 9 lines with em-dashes and the remaining instances are concentrated in the confession, painting, and ending scenes. Fail if density remains above 8%.

acceptance_test.excerpt:
3595: Dani picked up the second page, the one about the portrait, and read it with the same focused attention she brought to her own evaluations. The spring light from the window behind her caught the fine hairs along her temple, and Nick noticed it automatically, before the thought formed, before he could stop the part of his brain that had been scoring ambient illumination for fourteen months. The light was consistent. Single source, indirect, high color temperature. He registered the assessment and let it pass through him like weather.
3596: 
3597: "This is better than anything the rubric asks for," Dani said. "This is what the work should actually look like."
3598: 
3599: Nick didn't answer. He was looking at the stack — page after page of Bao's observations, each one a record of a man who had seen more than the system wanted from him and written it down anyway, knowing that no one would read it, knowing that his per-image time was already flagged, knowing that this precision was the thing that would cost him his job and doing it anyway because the alternative was seeing less than he could see. Bao's handwriting on the pages was steady and unhurried, the pen strokes of someone...

evidence.excerpt: 3569: Nick picked up the top sheet. Bao's handwriting was small and precise — a graphic designer's lettering, each character formed with the same attention he'd given to his evaluations. The note was about a training image, or had started that way. At the top he'd written the batch number and a description: *Garden scene, residential, midday sun, generated.* Below that, where the rubric asked for a rating and perhaps a brief comment, Bao had written three dense paragraphs about the quality of green in the foliage — how the model had defaulted to a single hue across every leaf when even a backyard garden in July held fifteen or twenty distinguishable greens, and how the shadow beneath the rosebush was blue-gray when it should have been a warm violet because the reflected light from the soil would tint it, and how the picket fence in the background was rendering as flat matte when any real painted wood in direct sun would show the grain and at least two values where the brush strokes caught the light differently.

3573: Nick turned to the next page. Another batch number, another image. This one described a portrait — *Woman, mid-thirties, indoor lighting, profile.* Bao's notes ran to the bottom of the page and continued on the back. He had written about the color of the woman's skin in shadow, how the model had grayed it out when real skin in shadow went warm — ochre or amber depending on the undertone — and how the ear, translucent at the portrait's angle against a window, should have shown the red of blood under cartilage, like a held-up hand against a strong lamp. He had written about the particular flatness of the generated hair, each strand identical in value, and the absence of the stray flyaway hairs that real indoor lighting caught and turned into small bright filaments against a dark background. At the bottom, another circled number: 3.

3599: Nick didn't answer. He was looking at the stack — page after page of Bao's observations, each one a record of a man who had seen more than the system wanted from him and written it down anyway, knowing that no one would read it, knowing that his per-image time was already flagged, knowing that this precision was the thing that would cost him his job and doing it anyway because the alternative was seeing less than he could see. Bao's handwriting on the pages was steady and unhurried, the pen strokes of someone who did not adjust his pace for anyone's metrics.

findings.problem: The chapter establishes Bao's handwritten evaluation notes through two detailed specimen passages (lines 3569-3573) that show what he wrote — green variations in foliage, skin color in shadow, flyaway hair filaments, each ending with a circled '3.' These passages earn the reader's understanding that Bao saw more than the system wanted and wrote it down anyway. Then lines 3595-3599 restate this meaning through Nick's internal narration: 'page after page of Bao's observations, each one a record of a man who had seen more than the system wanted from him and written it down anyway, knowing that no one would read it, knowing that his per-image time was already flagged, knowing that this precision was the thing that would cost him his job and doing it anyway because the alternative was seeing less than he could see.' The triple-knowing construction explicitly interprets what the notes already showed. The insight has been earned through concrete evidence; the narratorial summary paraphrases rather than extends it.
findings.rewrite_direction: Revise snapshots/cycle_02/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3595-3599. Cut or compress the explanatory narration so the chapter moves from the specimen notes directly into the confession attempt at line 3601. Options: (A) Delete lines 3595-3599 entirely and let the physical image of Nick holding the stack carry the emotional weight into the confession beat. (B) Reduce to a single sentence of physical observation — Nick holding the stack, the weight of the pages, the steadiness of Bao's handwriting — without restating the thematic meaning the notes already delivered. Do not let the narrator explain what Bao's notes mean when the notes themselves are on the page.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if revised chapter contains no more than 9 lines with em-dashes and the remaining instances are concentrated in the confession, painting, and ending scenes. Fail if density remains above 8%.

evidence.excerpt: 3565: When HR came back around about the workstation, Bao's desk was already mostly cleared — he'd done it himself that morning, stacking his things into the cardboard box with the same deliberate care he brought to everything. Dani had volunteered to finish clearing it out, Nick had said he'd help, and now they were here in the mid-afternoon quiet of the evaluation floor, pulling open drawers that Bao had already been through.

3599: Nick didn't answer. He was looking at the stack — page after page of Bao's observations, each one a record of a man who had seen more than the system wanted from him and written it down anyway, knowing that no one would read it, knowing that his per-image time was already flagged, knowing that this precision was the thing that would cost him his job and doing it anyway because the alternative was seeing less than he could see. Bao's handwriting on the pages was steady and unhurried, the pen strokes of someone who did not adjust his pace for anyone's metrics.

3741: He had set up to paint. He'd been sitting in front of the setup for forty minutes, which was less time than in December, when he'd lasted an hour before producing a technically competent and completely dead canvas he'd scraped off and thrown in the trash. The folding easel he'd bought at Blick Art Materials when he still thought of himself as someone who bought art supplies. A pre-stretched canvas, sixteen by twenty, a size he used to work at without thinking and now found large in a way he couldn't explain. The paints were on the side table in the order he'd always kept them: whites on the left, earths in the middle, cadmiums and phthalos on the right. A jar of turpentine he'd had to dig out from under the bathroom sink. Two brushes — both filbert, a six and a ten — pulled from the coffee can where they stood bristle-up like dead stems.

Dani had volunteered to finish clearing it out, Nick had said he'd help, and now they were here in the mid-afternoon quiet of the evaluation floor, pulling open drawers that Bao had already been through.
3566: 
3567: The top drawer held a phone charger that wasn't Bao's. It had been there since before he started, one of those universal artifacts of office furniture that belonged to no one and would outlast everyone. Dani set it on the desk. The second drawer was empty except for a handful of paper clips and a rubber band shaped like a figure eight. The third drawer was deeper, designed for hanging files, and when Nick pulled it open he found a stack of loose pages, handwritten, that Bao had either forgotten or decided didn't belong to him anymore.

findings.problem: Chapter 18 contains 41 em-dashes across 110 content lines (37.3%). The desk-clearing scene, Priya's confession, Marcus's meeting, and the final painting attempt all use em-dashes for description and apposition.
findings.rewrite_direction: Revise snapshots/cycle_02/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3563-3783. Reduce em-dashes from 41 to no more than 9. Keep em-dashes for the non-confession break ('I did,' he said, and stopped), the painting failure, and the final image loading. Strip from descriptive passages, office setup, and routine narration.

non_negotiables: Maintain heading contract exactly (# Chapter N).
Elliptical clipping is not permitted in dialogue or narration.
Avoid transcript cadence in high-stakes dialogue.
Avoid stiff over-formality in dialogue; use contractions and colloquial rhythm where character-true.
Preserve productive dialogue roughness; do not clean living speech into balanced thesis statements.
Reduce procedural drift when it displaces embodied consequence.
Maintain character-specific voice signatures from style bible.
Honor aesthetic risk policy: sanitization is the primary craft risk — do not soften, euphemize, or retreat from the story's established register. When in doubt, err toward rawness.

aesthetic_risk_policy.creative_risk_policy: This is a dark workplace comedy about AI, art, labor, and moral compromise. The novel's risks are not gratuitous — they emerge from the premise's pressures. Lean into: the specific humiliation of having your creative work rated by strangers on a five-point scale; the moral vertigo of befriending people you plan to betray; the comedy of a painter who is terrible at coding but too angry to quit; the economic desperation that makes gig work feel like a lifeline even when it is degrading; the creeping horror of watching your own vocabulary get overwritten by corporate evaluation language. Do not retreat from any of these. The biggest creative risk in this novel is not a single shocking scene but the sustained commitment to showing how a system designed to evaluate art eventually evaluates the evaluator — and finding that funny and devastating in the same breath.
aesthetic_risk_policy.euphemism_penalty: Euphemism for difficult content — economic shame, professional humiliation, moral compromise, grief, attraction, anger — is a craft failure equal to bad prose. When Nick's poverty is the subject, the prose must name amounts, count coins, and show the specific texture of not having enough. When Nick's sabotage causes harm, the prose must not retreat into abstraction. When characters are attracted to each other, the prose must inhabit the attraction physically and specifically. The novel's comedy does not exempt it from emotional specificity; if anything, the comic register demands more precision because the reader needs to feel the real thing beneath the joke.

dialogue_rules.default_contraction_use: High — contractions are the norm for all contemporary dialogue in this novel. Uncontracted forms should feel deliberate: emphasis, precision, formality, or a character slowing down to make sure they are heard. If a line reads stiffly and the character has no reason to be formal, contract it.

prose_style_profile.chapter_texture_variance: Alternate high-intensity chapters with reflective or quiet ones. Vary scene count, dialogue density, and pacing across the book. Comic chapters should have quicker pacing and more dialogue; quiet chapters should breathe with interiority and observation. No two consecutive chapters should have the same structural rhythm: if Chapter 7 is a multi-scene public chapter with confrontations and revelations, Chapter 8 should be a focused, inward chapter with fewer scenes and more technical observation. The middle third should resist the temptation to escalate continuously — build in genuine rest, meals, transit, and moments of ordinary life between plot advances.
prose_style_profile.diction: Contemporary American English. The base register is educated but not academic — a working painter's vocabulary, which includes both colloquial speech and precise visual-art terminology used without pretension. Tech-industry vocabulary enters through the workplace and should feel like a foreign language Nick gradually absorbs. The novel's linguistic arc: art vocabulary eroded by evaluation vocabulary eroded by corporate vocabulary, each layer burying the one before.
forbidden_drift_patterns: Prestige-literary inflation: do not upgrade the register when the scene is ordinary. A man eating cereal should not sound like a man contemplating mortality unless the text earns the escalation.
Irony saturation: the novel's comic register must coexist with genuine emotional presence. Do not let narrative irony become the default lens through which every moment is filtered.
Competence porn: Nick is good at visual evaluation, but the prose should not admire his skill. Show what he sees, not how impressive it is that he sees it.

Rubric-language comedy overuse: the colonization of Nick's vocabulary by evaluation terms is a real psychological event, not a running gag. Use it with increasing naturalness, not escalating self-awareness.
Clipped narration: do not drop subjects, articles, or auxiliaries for rhythm. Short complete sentences are fine; telegraphic fragments are not.

Sentimentalizing the coworkers: Bao, Dani, Tommy, and Priya carry genuine weight, but the prose must not soften them into objects of pity. They are people with their own lives, not casualties arranged to make Nick feel bad.
Over-explaining the comedy: if something is funny, the prose should trust the reader to laugh without narratorial wink, pause, or commentary.
prose_style_profile.narrative_distance: Close third person, locked to Nick Bray. The reader sees only what Nick sees, hears only what Nick hears. Nick's painter's eye shapes the narration: he notices light, color, spatial composition, anatomical detail, and surface texture before he notices emotion. His visual intelligence is the prose's dominant sensory engine. As the novel progresses, evaluation-rubric language infiltrates the narration — not as comic device but as genuine perceptual colonization. The rubric vocabulary should feel naturalized by mid-novel, not quoted.
prose_style_profile.rhythm_target: Variable across chapters and within chapters. Comic passages: quicker, punchier, shorter sentences with abrupt turns. Emotional passages: longer sentences, more subordinate clauses, slowed observation. Work passages: flat, repetitive, industrial rhythm that mirrors the labor. Do not default to one rhythm. The prose should shift gear to match what the chapter is doing.
sensory_bias: visual (dominant — Nick's painter's eye shapes all perception)
tactile (secondary — paint, keyboard, physical objects, temperature, texture of surfaces)
auditory (tertiary — the hum of monitors, Slack notifications, BART sounds, Oakland street noise)
